title: RoI Pooling Layer
concept: Region of Interest (RoI) Pooling extracts a fixed-size feature vector from any arbitrary rectangular section of the shared feature map.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How do we project original coordinates onto a shared feature map?

options.text: By dividing them by the backbone's downsampling factor
By multiplying them by the learning rate

exercises.explain: Scaling coordinates by the downsampling factor maps them onto the feature map.

cards.title: The Cookie Cutter
cards.body: If you have scraps of dough of all different shapes — some long, some wide, some tiny — and you want to bake identical cookies, you use a standard cookie cutter. It shapes any arbitrary piece of dough into a uniform grid size. That is what RoI Pooling does to feature regions.

cards.title: RoI Pooling
cards.body: RoI Pooling (Region of Interest Pooling) is a layer that downsamples any projected rectangular feature region of size $H imes W$ into a fixed, uniform output dimension (typically $7 imes 7$). It divides the region into a grid of sub-sections and performs max pooling in each.

cards.title: Splitting a Cake
cards.body: Imagine you have cakes of different rectangular sizes. You want to serve exactly 9 slices (a 3x3 grid) from each cake. If the cake is large, you make large slices. If the cake is small, you make tiny slices. Each slice represents a pooled grid cell!

cards.title: Max Pooling a Grid
cards.body: A projected region has size 14x21. We want a 7x7 output. RoI Pooling divides the region into 7x7 sub-grids, where each sub-grid is 2x3 features. It calculates the maximum value inside each 2x3 block, outputting a 7x7 grid.

cards.title: Not standard image resizing
cards.body: You might think RoI Pooling is just stretching pixels like resizing a photo. It actually operates on the feature activations directly, partitioning the math into grids and selecting the strongest signals (max values), preserving spatial activations.

cards.title: RoI Pooling Action
cards.body: Visualizing how a variable region is pooled into a uniform output:

left.text:
Proposed crops have different sizes:
e.g. 14x21 or 8x8 or 20x10

right.text:
Always outputs a fixed grid size:
e.g. 7x7 features

exercises.prompt: What is the primary function of the RoI Pooling layer?
exercises.explain: RoI Pooling takes rectangular regions of any dimensions and downsamples them to a fixed grid size (like 7x7) so they can feed into fully connected layers.

options.text: To convert arbitrary-sized region proposals on the feature map into a fixed-size vector
To generate 2,000 candidate proposals on the CPU
To classify the objects into binary SVM categories
To filter out weak background noise

exercises.prompt: True or False: RoI Pooling uses the average value of each sub-grid division to normalize features.
exercises.explain: False. RoI Pooling uses max pooling, which selects the maximum (strongest) feature value inside each grid cell division.
RoI Pooling uses max pooling to extract the strongest features from each grid section.
exercises.sentence: RoI Pooling divides a region into a grid and outputs the ___ activation in each grid section.

exercises.prompt: Which output format represents the result of a RoI Pooling layer?
exercises.explain: RoI Pooling outputs a standardized tensor (usually 7x7) for each proposed region.

options.text: A fixed-size feature activation map of shape [batch_size, channels, 7, 7]
A set of four corner pixel coordinates [xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax]

exercises.prompt: A projected region of interest on the feature map has a height of 21 and a width of 14. You apply a RoI Pooling layer configured to output a 7x7 grid. What are the dimensions of each grid cell (sub-section) that is max-pooled?
exercises.explain: Exactly! To divide a 21x14 region into 7x7 cells, you divide height by 7 (21 / 7 = 3) and width by 7 (14 / 7 = 2). Each cell is 3x2. Next: let's learn how Fast R-CNN trains classification and regression together!
